identity.title: Electric Lamp Bulb and Other Lighting Equipment Manufacturing

lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers of electric lamp bulbs and parts other than LED semiconductor devices, street and outdoor lighting equipment, flashlights, and other lighting equipment that repeatedly supply external OEM, municipal, distributor, private-label, and contract-manufacturing customers.
lens.excluded_activities: LED semiconductor manufacturers, residential and commercial fixture manufacturers classified separately, distributors, captive internal plants, firms outside the EBITDA band, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Predominantly per-unit or project product sales through distributors, OEMs, government buyers, and retailers; qualifying outsourced-service revenue is limited to recurring private-label, contract manufacturing, configuration, and lifecycle support.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations across NAICS 335 rather than wage-weighted tasks in LMM lighting-equipment firms. The range maps exposure to product configuration, optical and thermal design assistance, quoting, procurement, planning, forecasting, machine-vision review, documentation, compliance, and customer service, while assigning lower exposure to electronic and mechanical assembly, soldering, testing, packaging, maintenance, and material handling; the broad subsector may overweight motor and battery work and understate lighting design.
a.rationale: BLS reports 65,350 electrical and electronic equipment assemblers and 57,660 team assemblers in NAICS 335 in 2025, showing a substantial physical production core. AI can assist engineering and information workflows around production, but final assembly, photometric and electrical testing, and reliability accountability remain physical.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census across at least ten US LMM lamp and other-lighting manufacturers, separating engineering, optical design, sales, quoting, planning, procurement, SMT and assembly, testing, quality, maintenance, packaging, logistics, compliance, and service, with existing automation documented.
caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was located.
The code mixes legacy bulbs, portable lights, street lighting, and other equipment with very different labor and technology profiles.

rho.rationale: CAD, optical models, bills of material, orders, machine data, inspection images, test results, certifications, and warranty records support AI in design assistance, quoting, planning, predictive maintenance, quality support, and service. Implementation is limited by product safety and electromagnetic testing, reliability validation, supply-chain volatility, legacy systems, cybersecurity for connected products, and the cost of field failures or recalls.
rho.change_evidence: Two-year pre/post deployments for AI-assisted engineering, forecasting, component substitution, scheduling, machine vision, test analysis, predictive maintenance, regulatory documentation, and customer service, reporting labor, yield, escapes, returns, lead time, and overrides.
caveats: DOE evidence describes technology adoption and connected-lighting potential, not factory AI realization.
Low-volume specialty firms may lack sufficient labeled quality and warranty data.

e.rationale: Most firms manufacture and sell products rather than provide a recurring outsourced service. Private-label and contract manufacturers, or specialized suppliers with recurring configuration and lifecycle accountability, can qualify, but branded commodity sellers, captive plants, import-dependent assemblers, and product-only operations do not. The dataset provides no defensible LMM firm count, so this share cannot establish an addressable number of firms.
e.change_evidence: A complete US manufacturer census identifying normalized EBITDA, private-label and contract-manufacturing revenue, recurring external accounts, brand ownership, affiliate sales, domestic value added, owner dependence, certifications, warranties, and plant condition.
caveats: The frozen dataset declares n missing; no firm count was researched or substituted.
Eligibility is sensitive to whether repeat configured product supply is treated as outsourced service.

s5.bridge: Gallup found 22% of US employer-business owners across industries planned a sale or ownership transfer within five years. The range discounts intentions for failed sales, internal transfers, closures, product-line asset deals, and the unknown size of the eligible lighting population; mature ownership can increase exits, while import competition, technology obsolescence, and certification liabilities can turn exits into closures.
s5.rationale: Private specialty manufacturers can transfer, but changing LED technology, channel power, imports, warranty exposure, working capital, and product certification constrain financeable going-concern sales. With n missing, population-level transfer opportunity remains especially uncertain.
s5.change_evidence: Five to ten years of NAICS 335139 closed deals, product-line sales, closures, and owner changes from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, lighting-industry brokers, SBA loans, UCC records, and state registries, matched to eligibility and owner age.
caveats: Gallup is not industry- or size-specific and measures plans rather than completed transfers.
The missing firm-count denominator prevents a stable observed industry transfer rate.

q.rationale: Specialty performance, certifications, municipal specifications, brand, and connected controls can retain some gains, while bulbs and portable lights face transparent prices, imports, distributor and retailer bargaining, tenders, and rapid component cost pass-through. Five-year retention is therefore limited unless savings improve reliability, service, or differentiated functionality.
q.change_evidence: SKU- and customer-level price-cost-margin bridges before and after automation, including component cost changes, rebates, tenders, private-label resets, returns, warranty costs, volumes, channel inventory, and competitor pricing over three years.
caveats: No representative contract or pass-through dataset was located.
Retention differs sharply between commodity replacement lamps and specified outdoor or connected systems.

d5.bridge: DOE forecasts LED installation share and energy savings across the entire general-lighting market, not constant-quality demand for NAICS 335139 products or eligible LMM manufacturers. LED conversion and connected lighting support upgrade units but longer lifetimes reduce replacement frequency; exclusion of LED semiconductor manufacture and separate fixture codes can make market adoption overstate this code's capture.
d5.rationale: DOE projects LEDs at 84% of lighting installations by 2035 and large further energy savings, supporting continuing conversion and connected-lighting activity. However, LED longevity, saturation, fixture integration, imports, and migration of value into semiconductor and controls can reduce replacement lamp and standalone equipment volume, producing a below-current base over five years.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year US shipment forecasts by replacement bulb, street light, portable light, nonelectric fixture, and specialty equipment; installed-base age, failure rates, LED retrofit saturation, connected-control adoption, imports, channel inventory, and eligible manufacturers' order books.
caveats: DOE's forecast is market-wide and long-dated, not a six-digit output forecast.
The code boundary excludes important LED and fixture value pools, and classification may shift as products integrate.

o.rationale: Remaining physical lighting demand requires an accountable operator to design or configure, source electronics and housings, assemble, test photometric and electrical performance, document compliance, manage firmware where relevant, and honor warranties. Software can automate selection and controls, but cannot alone deliver certified physical products.
o.change_evidence: OEM and buyer sourcing data measuring shifts to imported finished products, software-defined lighting, modular commodity hardware, direct marketplace purchasing, and lights-out production, alongside certification and warranty accountability.
caveats: Accountable manufacturing can consolidate into large global suppliers outside the lens.
Connected lighting can move value from hardware operators to software platforms without eliminating physical products.

narrative.executive_view: Lighting equipment combines AI-addressable design, planning, quality, and service work with assembly-heavy physical production. The larger issue is fit: most firms sell products rather than an outsourced service, the LMM firm count is missing, and LED longevity can reduce replacement demand even as connected-lighting adoption grows.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist optical and thermal design, component substitution, quoting, forecasting, planning, inspection, test analysis, documentation, and customer support. Humans and equipment remain responsible for assembly, soldering, testing, packaging, certification, and release.
narrative.value_capture: Commodity lamps and portable products face imports, transparent pricing, tenders, and channel bargaining. Specialty, certified, municipal, and connected products can retain more through performance, reliability, service, and switching costs.
narrative.firm_availability: Only private-label, contract-manufacturing, or service-rich specialty suppliers clearly fit the lens. The frozen dataset has no defensible n, so even a bounded eligibility share cannot support a target count without a separate census.
narrative.demand_durability: LED conversion and connected lighting support upgrades, but longer product life and high eventual penetration reduce replacement frequency. Code boundaries also shift value toward semiconductors, controls, and fixture categories outside this industry.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are missing firm count, service-lens ineligibility, imports, technology obsolescence, falling replacement frequency, channel inventory, certification and warranty claims, component shortages, cybersecurity, and code-boundary leakage.
narrative.principal_driver: Digital engineering, quality, and connected-product workflows provide AI opportunities around a high labor-share manufacturing process.
narrative.principal_weakness: The eligible firm population is unknown and likely small, while LED longevity erodes replacement volume.

sources.title: Electrical Equipment, Appliance, and Component Manufacturing: NAICS 335
sources.supports: Reports 2025 broader-subsector employment including 65,350 electrical and electronic assemblers, 57,660 team assemblers, and 14,050 inspectors, and identifies electric lighting equipment as a NAICS 335 group.

sources.title: NAICS Classification for Electric Lighting Equipment Manufacturing
sources.supports: Places electric bulbs and tubes other than LED devices, street lights, flashlights, and nonelectric lighting equipment in NAICS 335139 while classifying LED devices in semiconductor manufacturing.

sources.title: Energy Savings Forecast of Solid-State Lighting
sources.supports: Projects LED lamps and luminaires at 84% of lighting applications by 2035 and 569 TWh of annual energy savings if DOE research goals are achieved; identifies connected lighting as part of projected savings.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Fall 2024 US survey found 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while 5% planned closure and 5% had no plan.

sources.title: About the Solid-State Lighting Program
sources.supports: States LED technology still has significant efficiency potential and projects 6.9 trillion kWh of cumulative electricity savings through 2035 if DOE targets for efficiency, controls, and connected lighting are met.
